# Provenance Guard

A backend service any creative-sharing platform can plug in to answer one question honestly:
**was this text written by a human, or generated by AI?** It classifies submitted text with two
independent signals, scores its confidence in a way that communicates uncertainty instead of
forcing a binary call, surfaces a plain-language transparency label to readers, and gives creators
a path to appeal a classification. Every decision and appeal is captured in a structured audit log,
and the submission endpoint is rate-limited.

> **Guiding principle:** on a creative platform, a *false positive* (labelling a human's work as AI)
> is worse than a false negative. The scoring, the label wording, and the appeals path are all
> tilted to avoid wrongly accusing a human.

The full design spec lives in [`planning.md`](planning.md); this README is the canonical record of
what was built and why.

---

## Setup & run

```bash
python -m venv .venv
source .venv/bin/activate          # Windows: .venv\Scripts\activate
pip install -r requirements.txt

# Provide a free Groq API key (https://console.groq.com) in .env:
#   GROQ_API_KEY=your_key_here

python app.py                      # serves on http://127.0.0.1:5000
```

> **macOS note:** on macOS, `localhost:5000` is intercepted by the AirPlay Receiver service (you'll
> get a `403` from `AirTunes`). Use `127.0.0.1:5000` instead, or disable *System Settings → General →
> AirDrop & Handoff → AirPlay Receiver*.

Test the two detection signals in isolation (no server needed for signal 2):

```bash
python tests/try_signal1.py        # LLM signal on 3 fixed texts (needs GROQ_API_KEY)
python tests/try_signal2.py        # stylometry signal on the same texts (pure Python)
python tests/try_scoring.py        # combined scoring on 4 calibration inputs
```

---

## API reference

| Endpoint | Body | Returns |
|----------|------|---------|
| `POST /submit` | `{ "text": str, "creator_id"?: str }` | `content_id`, `attribution`, `confidence`, `p_ai`, `label:{variant,text}`, `signals:{llm,stylometry}`, `status`. Rate-limited; `400` on empty/oversized text. |
| `POST /appeal` | `{ "content_id": str, "creator_reasoning": str }` | `appeal_id`, `content_id`, `status:"under_review"`, `message`. `404` unknown id, `400` empty reasoning. |
| `GET /log` | — | `{ "entries": [...] }`, newest first. |
| `GET /health` | — | `{ "status": "ok" }`. |

Example:

```bash
curl -s -X POST 127.0.0.1:5000/submit -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"text": "The sun dipped below the horizon...", "creator_id": "test-user-1"}'
```

---

## Architecture & design decisions

```
SUBMISSION FLOW
                         raw text            raw text
  client ──POST /submit──▶ app.py ──┬──────────────────▶ llm_signal (Groq)
                             │       │                      │ {p_ai, rationale}
                             │       └──────────────────▶ stylometry_signal
                             │                              │ {p_ai, features}
                             │        signal scores ────────┘
                             ▼
                          scoring.py ── p_ai + confidence ──▶ labels.py
                             │                                   │ label text
                             ├── audit entry ──▶ audit_log.jsonl │
                             ├── record ──▶ store (submissions.json)
                             ▼                                   │
  client ◀── JSON: {content_id, attribution, confidence, label, signals} ◀┘

APPEAL FLOW
  client ──POST /appeal──▶ app.py ──▶ store: status = "under_review"
   {content_id,               └──▶ audit_log.jsonl (appeal + orig decision)
    creator_reasoning}  ◀── JSON: {appeal_id, status:"under_review"} ──┘
```

A submission is fanned out to two independent detectors, their scores are combined into one
`p_ai` and a `confidence`, that verdict is mapped to a transparency label, and the whole decision
is logged before the response returns. An appeal flips the submission's status to `under_review`
and records the creator's reasoning alongside a snapshot of the original decision. Persistence is
plain JSON files (`data/submissions.json`, `data/appeals.json`) plus an append-only JSONL audit
log — deliberately simple, no database. Configuration (weights, thresholds, rate limits) lives in
one place, [`config.py`](config.py).

---

## Detection signals

The pipeline uses **two genuinely distinct signals** — one *semantic*, one *structural* — so their
combination is more informative than either alone.

### Signal 1 — LLM classifier (Groq `llama-3.3-70b-versatile`)
- **What it captures:** the holistic, semantic read — does the passage *sound* machine-generated
  (generic phrasing, even hedging, formulaic transitions, low idiosyncrasy)? Returns a calibrated
  0–1 probability plus a one-line rationale, parsed from a strict-JSON reply.
- **Why this signal:** modern AI text is fluent but bland in recognisable ways, and a strong LLM is
  the best available reader of that quality. It sees meaning the statistics can't.
- **Blind spot:** nondeterministic and can be overconfident; it struggles to tell dense *human*
  expert prose from AI (see Known limitations). On any API or parse failure it falls back to
  `p_ai = 0.5` ("can't tell") rather than guessing — the pipeline never invents a verdict.

### Signal 2 — Stylometric heuristics (pure Python, no dependencies)
Three structural properties, each independent of meaning:
- **Burstiness** — coefficient of variation of sentence length. Humans mix long and short sentences;
  AI is more uniform.
- **Type-token ratio (TTR)** — vocabulary diversity.
- **Punctuation density** — humans use varied marks (dashes, semicolons); AI trends plainer.

Each is normalised to a 0–1 "AI-likeness" and combined as a **weighted sum** — burstiness `0.5`,
punctuation `0.3`, TTR `0.2`.

- **Why this weighting:** during calibration, burstiness separated human/AI most cleanly, while TTR
  proved *directionally weak* — on short texts, formal AI prose is often **more** lexically diverse
  than casual human writing, so raw TTR pointed the wrong way. It's kept as a minor input, not
  dropped, but down-weighted.
- **Why this signal at all:** it's a cheap structural cross-check the LLM can't see, and it's fully
  transparent (every feature value is in the audit log).
- **Blind spot:** short texts are noisy (below 4 sentences the signal is blended back toward 0.5),
  and a formulaic human or a deliberately varied AI can invert the pattern. It never decides alone.

---

## Confidence scoring

**The number was designed before the math.** We first decided what it should *mean*, then built to
that:

- `p_ai` — combined probability the text is AI: `p_ai = 0.70·llm + 0.30·stylometry`. The LLM is
  weighted higher because it's the stronger reader; stylometry is the structural sanity check.
- **Disagreement damping** — if the two signals land on opposite sides of 0.5, `p_ai` is pulled
  gently back toward 0.5 (`0.5 + (p_ai−0.5)·0.85`). In practice this fires on the "LLM says AI /
  stylometry says human" pattern that *formal-but-human* prose produces, so it holds back borderline
  AI calls — the false-positive-averse direction.
- `confidence = max(p_ai, 1−p_ai)` — the probability mass behind whichever side we report. It ranges
  **0.5 (a coin toss) → 1.0 (certain)** and reads naturally: "Likely AI, confidence 71%" means
  `p_ai = 0.71`.
- **Verdict thresholds:** `p_ai ≥ 0.65` → *likely AI*; `p_ai ≤ 0.35` → *likely human*; the band
  between → **uncertain** (the default). A 0.55-confidence text is reported as *uncertain*, not
  forced into a side — that's the whole point.

### Where false-positive aversion lives
Not in the raw thresholds (they're symmetric) but in behaviour: the LLM prompt is explicitly told to
lean human when torn and not to flag dense human prose as AI merely for being formal, and the
disagreement damping mostly suppresses AI calls. When in doubt, the system says **uncertain**.

### How it was tested
The scoring was calibrated against four deliberately chosen inputs (clearly-AI, clearly-human, and
two borderline cases — see `tests/try_scoring.py`). The scores separate meaningfully and reach all
three label categories:

| Input | LLM | Stylometry | Combined `p_ai` | Confidence | Verdict |
|-------|-----|-----------|-----------------|-----------|---------|
| Clearly AI (paradigm-shift boilerplate) | 0.90 | 0.40 | **0.71** | 0.71 | likely_ai |
| Clearly human (casual ramen review) | 0.10 | 0.25 | **0.15** | 0.85 | likely_human |
| Borderline: formal human (economics) | 0.40 | 0.54 | **0.45** | 0.55 | uncertain |
| Borderline: lightly-edited AI (remote work) | 0.40 | 0.29 | **0.37** | 0.63 | uncertain |

### Two example submissions with meaningfully different confidence
- **High-confidence:** the casual human review → `likely_human`, **confidence 0.84** ("no strong
  signs of AI generation").
- **Lower-confidence:** the formal economics paragraph → `uncertain`, **confidence 0.55** (the
  signals disagreed and the system declined to make a call).

Same pipeline, ~0.30 apart in confidence and two different labels — the score is not a constant.

---

## Transparency label

The label shown to a reader changes with the verdict. All three variants (exact text, verbatim from
[`detection/labels.py`](detection/labels.py); `{NN}` is `confidence` as a whole percentage):

| Variant | Shown when | Exact text |
|---------|-----------|------------|
| **High-confidence AI** | `attribution = likely_ai` | ⚠️ Likely AI-generated. Our analysis strongly suggests this text was produced by an AI system. Confidence: {NN}%. The creator can appeal this assessment. |
| **High-confidence human** | `attribution = likely_human` | ✍️ Likely human-written. Our analysis found no strong signs of AI generation. Confidence: {NN}%. |
| **Uncertain** | `attribution = uncertain` | ❓ Uncertain origin. Our analysis couldn't reliably determine whether this text is human- or AI-written, so we're not making a call. Treat attribution as unconfirmed. |

Design choices: the AI label says "*suggests*" and offers the appeal in the same breath — it never
states AI authorship as fact. Only the two confident labels show a percentage; the uncertain label
deliberately omits a number, because surfacing "55%" would imply false precision to a lay reader.
All three were confirmed reachable by live submissions (see the calibration table above).

---

## Appeals workflow

A creator who believes they were misclassified `POST`s to `/appeal` with the `content_id` and free-text
`creator_reasoning`. The endpoint:
1. Verifies the submission exists (`404` if not).
2. Flips its status `classified` → **`under_review`**.
3. Writes an appeal record and appends an **appeal** entry to the audit log, including the creator's
   reasoning and a snapshot of the original verdict + confidence.
4. Returns a confirmation. **No automated re-classification** — a human reviews contested content.

```bash
curl -s -X POST 127.0.0.1:5000/appeal -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"content_id": "sub_9a7bdb15147f", "creator_reasoning": "I wrote this myself..."}'
# -> {"appeal_id":"apl_a2b82220600a","content_id":"sub_9a7bdb15147f",
#     "status":"under_review","message":"Your appeal has been logged..."}
```

A human reviewer opening `GET /log` sees, per appeal: the creator's reasoning, the original verdict +
confidence + `p_ai`, and both signal scores — enough to judge the original call without re-running
detection.

---

## Rate limiting

`Flask-Limiter` (in-memory store), per client IP:

| Endpoint | Limit |
|----------|-------|
| `POST /submit` | **10 / minute** and **100 / day** |
| `POST /appeal` | 20 / hour |
| `GET /log`, `GET /health` | unlimited (read-only) |

**Reasoning:** a real creator submits a handful of pieces a day and may retry a few times in quick
succession, so **10/min** covers legitimate bursts without friction; **100/day** sits far above human
use but throttles an adversary trying to flood the classifier — and each `/submit` is a paid Groq
call, so the daily cap protects cost as well as availability. Appeals are rare events, so 20/hour
stops abuse while never blocking a genuine dispute.

**Evidence** — 12 rapid `POST /submit` requests (limit is 10/min):

```
req 1 -> 200      req 5 -> 200      req 9  -> 200
req 2 -> 200      req 6 -> 200      req 10 -> 200
req 3 -> 200      req 7 -> 200      req 11 -> 429
req 4 -> 200      req 8 -> 200      req 12 -> 429
```

The `429` body:

```json
{ "error": "Rate limit exceeded. Please slow down and try again later.",
  "detail": "10 per 1 minute" }
```

---

## Audit log

Append-only JSONL (`data/audit_log.jsonl`), surfaced via `GET /log`. Every decision records the
timestamp, content id, attribution, combined confidence, **both individual signal scores**
(`llm_score`, `stylometry_score`) and the full feature breakdown; every appeal records the reasoning
and a snapshot of the original decision. Sample entries (real output):

**Decision entry:**
```json
{
  "event": "decision",
  "content_id": "sub_9a7bdb15147f",
  "creator_id": "user-ai",
  "timestamp": "2026-07-06T01:21:19.170540+00:00",
  "attribution": "likely_ai",
  "confidence": 0.7127,
  "p_ai": 0.7127,
  "llm_score": 0.9,
  "stylometry_score": 0.4007,
  "status": "classified",
  "signals": {
    "llm": { "p_ai": 0.9, "rationale": "The text features generic phrasing and formulaic transitions." },
    "stylometry": { "p_ai": 0.4007, "features": {
      "n_words": 43, "n_sentences": 3, "sentence_length_cv": 0.3793,
      "type_token_ratio": 0.8837, "punctuation_density": 0.0465 } }
  }
}
```

**Human decision entry (abridged):**
```json
{ "event": "decision", "content_id": "sub_a6b03746e9b7", "attribution": "likely_human",
  "confidence": 0.84, "p_ai": 0.16, "llm_score": 0.1, "stylometry_score": 0.3, "status": "classified" }
```

**Appeal entry:**
```json
{
  "event": "appeal",
  "appeal_id": "apl_a2b82220600a",
  "content_id": "sub_9a7bdb15147f",
  "timestamp": "2026-07-06T01:21:54.590519+00:00",
  "status": "under_review",
  "appeal_reasoning": "I wrote this myself from personal experience. I am a non-native English speaker and my writing style may appear more formal than typical.",
  "original_decision": { "attribution": "likely_ai", "confidence": 0.7127, "p_ai": 0.7127 }
}
```

---

## Known limitations

**Dense, formal human writing (academic / technical / non-native-speaker prose) is the case the
system is most likely to get wrong.** This is not a data problem — it's a property of both signals:
- The **stylometry** signal reads uniform sentence rhythm and safe punctuation as AI-like.
- The **LLM** signal genuinely cannot distinguish substantive human expert prose from AI boilerplate
  — in testing, the formal economics paragraph drew the same ~0.8 AI score from the LLM as clearly-AI
  text until the prompt was tuned to treat domain-dense content as more human.

The system's response is to hedge: such text lands in **uncertain** rather than a false accusation,
and the creator can appeal. But a reader gets less useful information for exactly the writers (formal,
non-native) who can least afford a wrong "AI" flag. **Lightly-edited AI** (human polish over an AI
draft) is the other fundamental blind spot — editing smooths away the very fingerprints both signals
rely on, so it typically reads as uncertain or human. Perfect AI detection is unsolved; the honest
move is to say so and provide the appeal path.

**If deploying for real** I'd add per-creator history (repeat patterns are more informative than a
single sample), a calibration set to tune thresholds against ground truth, and a persistent store
with authentication on `/appeal` and `/log`.

---

## Spec reflection

- **How the spec helped:** writing the "what does the confidence number *mean*" section in
  `planning.md` *before* coding forced the decision that `0.5 = "can't tell"` and that *uncertain*
  is the default verdict. When the implementation later fought me, that fixed meaning was the anchor
  that kept the design honest instead of drifting toward a forced binary.
- **How the implementation diverged, and why:** the spec originally set an AI threshold of `0.80`
  with a hard "signals must agree" rule, and defined confidence as *distance from 0.5*. In
  calibration this made the "Likely AI" label **unreachable** — clearly-AI text stalled at
  `uncertain` because stylometry structurally disagrees with the LLM on formal prose. I lowered the
  threshold to `0.65`, dropped the "AND agree" blocker in favour of gentle disagreement *damping*,
  and redefined confidence as *probability of the predicted class* (so a "Likely AI" label doesn't
  absurdly display "32%"). `planning.md` was updated to match. The design *goal* (false-positive
  aversion) survived; the *mechanism* moved from rigid thresholds to calibrated behaviour.

---

## AI usage

This project was built with AI assistance (Claude), directed against the `planning.md` spec.

1. **Stylometry signal + feature weighting.** I directed the AI to generate `stylometry_signal.py`
   from the spec's signal-2 section (burstiness, TTR, punctuation → one score). It initially averaged
   the three features equally. Testing showed the type-token ratio was pointing the *wrong way* on
   short formal text (AI scored *more* diverse than casual human), so I overrode the equal average
   with a documented weighted sum (burstiness 0.5 / punctuation 0.3 / TTR 0.2) and added a
   sub-4-sentence reliability hedge.

2. **Confidence scoring + calibration.** I directed the AI to generate `scoring.combine()` to the
   spec's thresholds. The generated function matched the spec — but running it against the four
   calibration inputs revealed the spec itself was miscalibrated (clearly-AI never reached the AI
   label). I iterated with the AI on the fix: reweighting the signals, softening the damping,
   revising the LLM prompt to be decisive on clear cases yet lean human on dense prose, and
   redefining the confidence metric — verifying after each change that all three labels became
   reachable and the four inputs matched intuition.

---

## Portfolio walkthrough

*(A short recorded walkthrough accompanies this project.)* It shows a submission flowing end-to-end
through both signals to a transparency label, an appeal moving a decision to `under_review`, the
rate limiter returning `429`, and the audit log — while talking through the confidence-scoring and
false-positive design decisions above.
